hero.bio: I care about the person behind the data — and building things that support and adjust to their needs, whether that's through machine learning or human-centered design.
hero.currently: OAK Lab Research Assistant; DFA Team Lead

professional: I am a junior at Carnegie Mellon University studying Statistics and Machine Learning, interested in how models can adapt to individuals rather than optimizing for the average user. My work sits at the intersection of quantitative research, where I study patterns in how people think and learn, and human-centered design, where I build to serve that individuality.

Currently, I am researching at the OAK Lab, where I build ML pipelines to extract features from learners' strategies and use statistical analyses to understand what that reveals about individual learning. With Design for America, I have worked directly with nonprofit partners in Pittsburgh, conducting user research, prototyping, and iterating on products through rounds of client feedback to translate their needs into something useful.
I'm looking broadly across DS/ML/AI/UX internships for Summer 2027. You can check out the Projects and Research tabs for my work!
funFacts: 🎤 VP of Internal Affairs for C#, CMU's largest singing organization
🎧 My favorite music genres are musical theater and K-pop!

research.title: Inside blocked practice: Verbal evidence for the attentional mechanisms driving category learning
research.authors: Julia J. Conti, Abigail Lo, Ken R. Koedinger, Paulo F. Carvalho
research.desc: Blocked presentation of information is thought to provide benefits over interleaving by directing attention to commonalities. However, most current evidence is indirect, in the form of posttest performance and eyetracking. In this study, participants learned to classify bugs organized around a relational rule in a 2x2 design manipulating blocked vs. interleaved presentation and example presence vs. absence. Throughout the task, we ask participants to make predictions about the organizing rule. Blocked presentation promoted better post-test performance and more relational feature verbalizations than interleaved; removing examples amplified feature use in both conditions. Moreover, greater increases in relational verbalizations during training positively predicted posttest performance, particularly for blocked learners, suggesting presentation format shapes reasoning trajectories.
